votes.rationale: Root cause is correctly diagnosed and the fix is at the right layer. CI invokes the bare `pytest -q` console script (.github/workflows/ci.yml:44 and :110), which—unlike `python -m pytest`—does not prepend CWD to sys.path. Six test modules import the `tests` package (`from tests import ...` / `from tests.integration.conftest import ...`), so under bare pytest collection failed with `ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'tests'`, erroring the whole test step. The fix (`pythonpath = ["src", "."]` in pyproject [tool.pytest.ini_options]) puts the repo root on sys.path for any invocation. I empirically verified discrimination by reverting to `pythonpath = ["src"]`: bare `pytest -q --co` then errored on collection with `No module named 'tests'` and the regression test FAILED; restoring the fix yields a clean collection of 560 tests and the test PASSES. The regression test test_pytest_path_includes_repo_root_so_tests_package_imports (tests/test_supply_chain.py:358) asserts `"." in pythonpath` and correctly fails when the fix is reverted. (An initial single-test run reported a transient failure that did not reproduce on a clean/no-cache run or in any subsequent run; the parsed config is unambiguously ['src','.'].) Fix is both appropriate and complete; no residual gap.
concerns: Minor (not a gap): the regression test asserts the static config value '.' is present in pythonpath rather than spawning the bare `pytest` console script and asserting the `tests` package actually imports. It is a faithful proxy since the config IS the fix mechanism, and I independently confirmed the runtime behavior, but an end-to-end subprocess assertion would be marginally stronger against future regressions (e.g., someone disabling pythonpath via another mechanism).

votes.rationale: Root cause correctly identified and fixed at the right layer. CI still invokes the bare `pytest` console script (ci.yml line 59: `pytest -q --cov=...`), which—unlike `python -m pytest`—does not prepend CWD to sys.path; suites doing `from tests import ...` failed at collection. The fix sets pythonpath = ["src", "."] in pyproject [tool.pytest.ini_options], making the `tests` package importable under ANY invocation. This is the correct, durable approach (vs. the brittle alternative of changing CI to `python -m pytest`). Verification: with the fix, bare `pytest -q --co` collects 560 tests cleanly and the regression test passes. Discrimination proven: reverting to pythonpath=["src"] makes bare pytest fail collection with exactly `ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'tests'` (5 collection errors) AND the regression test fails (`assert '.' in ['src']`). Fix is both appropriate and complete.
concerns: Non-blocking robustness note: the regression test asserts the pyproject config invariant ("." present in pythonpath) rather than driving an actual subprocess bare-`pytest` collection. It pins the exact knob and catches a revert of THIS fix, but would not catch a future regression where collection breaks for a different reason (e.g., conftest sys.path manipulation removed) while "." remains. Direct empirical check confirms real collection works, so this is not a residual defect for K-CI-2.

votes.rationale: K-CI-1 is correctly diagnosed and fixed. Root cause reproduced directly: seam.py has a guarded `import httpx` (lines 356-363) used to build a per-phase httpx.Timeout for the BULK tier; httpx is only a (transitive) dependency of the reasoning extra and is absent from requirements-core.lock, which backs the CI `core` lane (ci.yml line 17, installs dev-only lock then runs `mypy` at line 42). With httpx unresolvable, mypy --strict errors `Cannot find implementation or library stub for module named "httpx" [import-not-found]` — I reproduced this exactly by running mypy --strict against an empty venv lacking httpx. The fix adds `"httpx.*"` to the existing `[[tool.mypy.overrides]]` ignore_missing_imports list, the same pattern as the 11 other optional backends; this is the correct layer (mypy config, not source) and is harmless in the integration lane where httpx is present (py.typed shipped, so mypy still type-checks the real module). mypy --strict now passes (Success: no issues found in 40 source files). The regression test discriminates: removing `"httpx.*"` makes test_mypy_ignores_httpx_missing_stub_in_core_lane fail with the documented assertion message; restoring it passes. pyproject.toml restored to committed state after the discrimination test.
concerns: Test fidelity (not a fix defect): the regression test is a config-string assertion — it tomllib-parses pyproject and asserts 'httpx.*' is in the ignore_missing_imports module list. It does NOT invoke mypy --strict in an httpx-free environment, so it tautologically tracks the fix's literal text rather than the no-stub behavior. It would not catch a NEW guarded optional import added to seam.py breaking the core lane in the same way. The real end-to-end protection is the CI core lane running `mypy`; the unit test is consistent with sibling supply-chain config tests but does not independently exercise the failure mode.

votes.rationale: Root cause is correctly identified: seam.py:357 has a guarded `import httpx` (used for the BULK-tier httpx.Timeout, with an ImportError float fallback). httpx ships only with the reasoning extra and is confirmed absent from requirements-core.lock (the no-extras core CI lane), so bare `mypy --strict` there raised `import-not-found` for httpx. The fix — adding "httpx.*" to the existing [[tool.mypy.overrides]] ignore_missing_imports block — is at the right layer and follows the identical established pattern used for the other optional/guarded backends (langchain_ollama, claude_agent_sdk, opentelemetry, etc.). I proved the fix is both necessary and sufficient by simulating the real core-lane condition: my dev venv contained httpx (masking the bug), so I physically hid the httpx package and re-ran mypy. WITH the override: 'Success: no issues found in 40 source files'. WITHOUT the override (httpx still hidden): the exact K-CI-1 error `seam.py:357: error: Cannot find implementation or library stub for module named "httpx" [import-not-found]`. The regression test passes as-is and fails when the httpx.* line is reverted, so it discriminates. All temporary state (httpx package, pyproject.toml) was restored and verified clean.
concerns: The regression test is a static config-parse assertion (checks `httpx.*` is in the ignore list); it does not actually invoke mypy with httpx absent. It correctly couples to this fix and fails on revert, but it would not catch a future regression where httpx leaks into the core lock or mypy import semantics change. This matches the style of the sibling supply-chain tests and is acceptable, not blocking.

votes.rationale: Root cause is correctly identified and fixed at the right layer: the lockfile-sync gate re-resolved against live PyPI with no point-in-time pin, so any in-range transitive publish flipped the diff gate red on clean checkouts. Fix adds --exclude-newer to all three CI compile commands AND to regenerate_lockfiles.sh, sourcing the cutoff from a single committed file (.uv-exclude-newer, 2026-06-14T00:00:00Z) parsed identically in both places — eliminating CI/script drift. Empirically verified reproducibility with uv 0.11.21: fresh `uv pip compile --exclude-newer` for requirements.lock, requirements-core.lock, and requirements-build.lock all diff MATCH (non-comment content) against the committed locks; running regenerate_lockfiles.sh from an unrelated cwd produces zero git drift on all three (the script correctly cds to repo root). The cutoff (2026-06-14) sits clear of the arize-phoenix 17.6/17.7 boundary. Regression tests test_lockfile_sync_is_resolution_pinned and test_regenerate_script_covers_all_gated_lockfiles pass and discriminate: removing --exclude-newer from ci.yml or deleting the date file fails the first; dropping the build lock or the flag from the script fails the second.
concerns: Non-blocking, named: the regression tests are config-level string-presence assertions (flag present, date file exists/well-formed, build lock referenced). They do NOT assert reproducibility itself — i.e. that the committed locks actually re-resolve to identical content at the pinned date. A dependency bump committed without regenerating the locks would pass these unit tests and be caught only by the live CI diff gate (which remains intact). Consistent with the file's stated config-level guard philosophy and acceptable, but a real limit on test discrimination scope.

votes.rationale: Root cause is correctly identified and fixed at the right layer. The lockfile sync gate previously ran `uv pip compile` with no `--exclude-newer`, resolving newest in-range transitives at wall-clock time, so the "in sync" gate went red the instant any unconstrained dep published — the exact described clean-checkout failure. The fix pins resolution to a committed point-in-time index (.uv-exclude-newer = 2026-06-14T00:00:00Z) read by BOTH ci.yml (all three compile commands) and scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh via identical `tr -d '[:space:]'` parsing, so CI and the script cannot drift. I empirically verified reproducibility with uv 0.11.21: all three locks (requirements.lock, requirements-core.lock, requirements-build.lock) regenerate byte-identical (non-comment content) at the committed cutoff. The date file is genuinely committed (git ls-files + git show on the fix commit). Discrimination verified: simulating a revert (stripping --exclude-newer from ci.yml + removing the date file) makes test_lockfile_sync_is_resolution_pinned FAIL; with the fix it passes. Working tree restored clean afterward; both regression tests pass.
concerns: Test is structural only: it asserts the substring --exclude-newer appears somewhere in ci.yml (not in each of the three compile commands) and that the date file exists/starts with a digit. It does not execute a uv compile to prove reproducibility, nor assert CI and the regen script reference the SAME date file. A future edit that dropped the flag from only one of the three CI commands, or hardcoded a divergent date in one place, would not be caught by this test. This is a test-strength limitation, not a defect in the K-OPS-1 fix; reproducibility was confirmed manually here.

The cutoff is static, so in-range releases/advisories published after 2026-06-14 are not picked up until the date is bumped — the intended, documented tradeoff (Dependabot handles advisory monitoring).

votes.rationale: Root cause is correctly identified: the SIGTERM handler replaced SIG_DFL's terminate-the-process default with flush-and-return, so when no prior Python handler existed the process stopped dying on SIGTERM (docker stop/kill/k8s hang until SIGKILL). The fix is at the right layer (inside _handler) and uses the canonical re-delivery idiom: re-establish SIG_DFL then os.kill(getpid, signum). Resetting to SIG_DFL before the re-kill avoids recursion back into _handler. Callable-prior chaining is preserved; SIG_IGN correctly falls through both branches and stays ignored. The regression test DISCRIMINATES: I reverted the fix to the bare `if callable(prior)` and test_sigterm_handler_redelivers_default_termination failed (asserting both the SIG_DFL re-establish and the os.kill re-delivery with correct signum); with the fix in place it passes. Full observability suite: 26 passed, 2 skipped. The pre-existing wiring test was correctly updated to tolerate the new reset-to-default signal.signal call.

Root cause is correctly diagnosed and fixed at the right layer. A Python SIGTERM handler that flushes-and-returns replaces the kernel's default terminate action; when the prior disposition was SIG_DFL (the common case for a fresh process), the original `if callable(prior)` branch never matched, so the handler returned without terminating — docker stop/kill/k8s would hang until SIGKILL. The fix re-establishes SIG_DFL via signal.signal(SIGTERM, SIG_DFL) then os.kill(getpid(), signum) to re-raise, letting the default terminate action run. This is the canonical re-delivery idiom. The callable-prior branch correctly chains a pre-existing handler; SIG_IGN intentionally falls through (no elif), preserving the ignore semantics — matching the original disposition. The comparison prior == signal.SIG_DFL is sound (getsignal returns the signal.Handlers enum member that == signal.SIG_DFL). os and signal are both imported. Discrimination verified empirically: I reverted the fix to the bare `if callable(prior): prior(...)` and test_sigterm_handler_redelivers_default_termination FAILED ('did not re-establish default SIGTERM disposition' — calls['reset'] empty); restoring the fix, all 26 observability tests pass (2 skipped). The new test asserts both the reset-to-SIG_DFL and the os.kill re-delivery with SIGTERM, so it catches a regression of either half. The existing wiring test was also updated to stub os.kill so it no longer signals the test process. Working tree restored clean after the revert experiment.

Fix is appropriate and complete. Root cause verified directly: freeze() admits hashable-but-non-JSON leaves (bytes/datetime), and plain json.dumps over entries_to_records raises TypeError on them, while json.dumps(..., default=str) succeeds. The fix in pipeline.py _log_trail (lines 756-774) does exactly the right three things at the right layer: (1) serializes the payload up front with default=str so the TypeError can no longer fire mid-write inside the NamedTemporaryFile(delete=False) block; (2) restructures so fh.name is always assigned the moment the file exists, making the finally/os.unlink cleanup reachable (no orphaned temp file); (3) adds _log.warning in track() (lines 328-332) so a swallowed tracker failure is no longer silent. The regression test DISCRIMINATES: I reverted only the _log_trail body to the pre-fix with-block form and the test failed with 'provenance artifact was silently dropped on non-JSON evidence (#K-OBS-2)' (assert prov == []), and it passes on the restored fixed code (1 passed). The test independently exercises all three guarantees — artifact attaches (prov non-empty), temp file readable during log_artifact but absent afterward (no leak), and the bytes value preserved as a string in the artifact content. File restored to HEAD (git diff clean).
concerns: Non-blocking, out of scope for K-OBS-2: ResultBundle.persist_trail (pipeline.py:241) still calls json.dumps(entries_to_records(...)) WITHOUT default=str, so it would raise TypeError on the same bytes/datetime leaf. This is a separate caller-invoked public API that raises visibly to the caller with no temp-file leak and no silent drop, so it is a different code path rather than a residual gap in the _log_trail fix — but it is the same latent serialization fragility and could merit its own finding.

votes.rationale: Fix targets the correct root cause: freeze_mapping only requires hashability, so bytes/datetime/Enum leaves enter the payload but break json serialization. Old code called json.dump inside the NamedTemporaryFile(delete=False) block before `path = fh.name`, so a TypeError orphaned the temp file (cleanup unreachable) and was swallowed by track(), silently dropping the #G-009 artifact. The fix is at the right layer (the _log_trail/track seam) and sound: (1) serialize up front with json.dumps(..., default=str) so it cannot raise mid-write and non-JSON values stringify instead of dropping; (2) `fh.name` is always assigned before the try/finally, making os.unlink cleanup always reachable; (3) _log.warning in track() so tracker failures are no longer silent. Verified empirically: the test passes on fixed code (1 passed). Reverting only the temp-file block (restoring the old with/json.dump/path=fh.name form) makes the test FAIL with 'provenance artifact was silently dropped on non-JSON evidence', driven by 'TypeError: Object of type bytes is not JSON serializable' — so the test genuinely discriminates. It asserts all three properties: artifact attaches, no temp-file leak, and the non-JSON value survives serialized; the log_artifact callback also asserts os.path.exists to catch early deletion. Original file restored, git diff clean.
concerns: Non-blocking: on the revert path the `assert prov` (artifact-dropped) assertion trips first, so the temp-leak and contents assertions are not independently exercised by that particular revert. They are nonetheless real, correctly-formed checks against the fix's other two properties, and the artifact-drop assertion alone is fully discriminating.

votes.rationale: Root cause is correct: require_local_uri rejected libpq Unix-domain-socket hosts (leading '/', e.g. host=/var/run/postgresql) as non-loopback, even though such a connection never touches the network and is strictly more data-sovereign than the TCP loopback the guard already permits. The fix adds a focused is_local_socket_path() predicate (accepts a single leading '/', rejects UNC '//' and '/\\') and applies it at exactly the two libpq host/hostaddr read points: the URI ?host=/hostaddr= query branch and the keyword/value DSN branch. Right layer, minimal, consistent with the existing bare-path UNC handling. Discrimination verified empirically: I reverted the two 'and not is_local_socket_path(h)' guards and the three accept-tests (host=/var/run/postgresql, host=/tmp dbname=assay, postgresql:///assay?host=/var/run/postgresql) all FAILED with NonLocalEndpointError; restored, all pass. The reject-tests (host=//evil/share, host=/\\evil/share) still raise, and the pre-existing UNC bare-path tests (//evil/share, /\\evil/share, etc.) still pass. Unit-level edge checks on is_local_socket_path confirm correct handling of empty/None/localhost/IP/whitespace. Full tests/test_persistence.py: 78 passed, 2 skipped.
concerns: Benign (not a defect): hostaddr=/var/run/postgresql is now also accepted even though libpq only accepts numeric IPs for hostaddr; a '/'-rooted value is non-routable/local so it cannot exfiltrate, and the guard's purpose (reject off-box endpoints) is still satisfied.

votes.rationale: Fix correctly addresses the root cause: libpq leading-'/' hosts are Unix-domain-socket directories (never touch the network) and were wrongly rejected as non-loopback. New is_local_socket_path() accepts a single leading '/' and rejects UNC prefixes (//, /\), applied at the right layer in BOTH branches a libpq socket host can appear (URI ?host=/hostaddr= query branch and keyword/value DSN branch) and correctly NOT in the URI-authority branch (a Unix socket never appears there). Tests discriminate: forcing is_local_socket_path to return False (reverting the fix) makes all three accept-cases (host=/var/run/postgresql, 'host=/tmp dbname=assay', postgresql:///assay?host=/var/run/postgresql) raise NonLocalEndpointError, while the UNC-lookalike reject-cases (host=//evil/share, host=/\evil/share) stay rejected. Verified the URI-query accept-case routes through the empty-netloc query branch (so it exercises the query-branch fix). Network hosts (evil.com, 8.8.8.8) remain rejected in both branches, and a mixed 'hostaddr=8.8.8.8 host=/tmp' still fails closed on the network hostaddr. Full persistence module: 78 passed, 2 skipped.

Root cause is correctly identified and fixed at the right layer. scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh previously regenerated only requirements.lock and requirements-core.lock (zero references to requirements-build.lock, confirmed via git show d08f031~1), while CI's audit job gates all three (ci.yml lines 154-157 compile and diff requirements-build.lock, failing red on staleness). A maintainer following the documented script on a build-backend bump stayed red with no scripted fix. The fix adds the build-lock compile step using the IDENTICAL invocation CI runs: `printf 'hatchling\neditables\n' > /tmp/build-reqs.in; uv pip compile /tmp/build-reqs.in --universal --generate-hashes --exclude-newer "$EXCLUDE_NEWER" -o requirements-build.lock`. I reproduced this command (exclude-newer=2026-06-14T00:00:00Z from .uv-exclude-newer) with uv 0.11.21 and the regenerated build lock diffs CLEAN against the committed requirements-build.lock — so script output == CI's regen == committed file. CI error messages for all three locks point at scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh (lines 146/151/157), and dependabot.yml references the script. The test test_regenerate_script_covers_all_gated_lockfiles passes; against the pre-fix script (no build-lock reference) its loop assertion would have failed, so it discriminates against the actual historical regression. Full 40-test supply-chain suite passes.
concerns: Test strength: test_regenerate_script_covers_all_gated_lockfiles only asserts substring presence of 'requirements-build.lock' in the script text. The fix introduced THREE mentions (comment line 20, the load-bearing `-o` compile command line 40, and the echo line 42). A partial revert that deleted only the executable `uv pip compile ... -o requirements-build.lock` line while leaving the comment or echo would leave the test green yet the script non-functional. It is a config-presence guard, not behavioral (it does not run the script and diff the output against the committed lock, the way the CI gate does). This discriminates against the exact historical bug (which had zero mentions) but not against every revert of the functional line. Minor robustness gap, not a residual functional gap.

votes.rationale: Root cause correct: regenerate_lockfiles.sh regenerated only requirements.lock + requirements-core.lock, while CI's audit job gates a THIRD lock, requirements-build.lock (ci.yml lines 153-157), leaving a maintainer red on a build-backend bump with no scripted fix. Verified the pre-fix script (HEAD~1) had ZERO references to requirements-build.lock. The fix adds the build-lock regen using the EXACT CI invocation (printf 'hatchling\neditables\n' > /tmp/build-reqs.in; uv pip compile ... --exclude-newer "$EXCLUDE_NEWER") at script lines 38-40, and points all three stale-lock CI errors at the script (ci.yml lines 146/151/157). Right layer (the documented remediation + CI error text), sound approach (mirror CI byte-for-byte; also added cd to repo root so relative paths resolve). Completeness verified empirically: regenerated the build lock with uv 0.11.21 at the committed exclude-newer date (2026-06-14T00:00:00Z) and diffed against committed requirements-build.lock — only the stripped header (-o path) differs, body identical, so CI's build-lock sync gate (which greps out '^#') passes. Discrimination verified: reverted scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh to the HEAD~1 buggy form and test_regenerate_script_covers_all_gated_lockfiles FAILED with the exact 'does not regenerate requirements-build.lock (#K-OPS-2)' assertion, then passed on restore.
concerns: Minor (non-blocking): the regression test is string-presence only — it asserts the three lock filenames and --exclude-newer appear in the script, but does not assert the script's build-lock INVOCATION matches CI's (same hatchling/editables inputs). A future drift between the two build-lock invocations would not be caught by this test; it is currently caught only by the live CI sync gate. Consistent with the existing test style and not a residual gap for K-OPS-2 itself.

votes.rationale: Root cause correct and fix at the right layer. The CI integration job at .github/workflows/ci.yml:110 now runs `pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing --cov-fail-under=90`; previously it had no threshold so `pytest --cov` exited 0 at any coverage (vacuous gate). The floor (90) is present and sits below actual coverage. I ran the full suite with the floor: `Required test coverage of 90% reached. Total coverage: 96.58%` — 555 passed, 5 skipped, ~6.6pp headroom. The regression test tests/test_supply_chain.py::test_coverage_floor_is_enforced reads the real CI file via _ci_text()/_CI and asserts `--cov-fail-under=` is present. It DISCRIMINATES: removing the flag from ci.yml flips it to FAIL (`coverage measured but not enforced`), and it passes with the flag restored. Both appropriateness and completeness criteria are met.
concerns: Minor (non-blocking): the regression test only asserts the substring `--cov-fail-under=` is present, not that the floor value is meaningful. A degenerate `--cov-fail-under=0` would still pass the test while restoring vacuous behavior. Actual CI value is 90 (verified), so this is a latent test-strength gap, not a current defect.

votes.rationale:
The fix correctly addresses the root cause of K-OPS-3. The vacuous gate was `pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing` (CI line 110), which prints coverage but exits 0 at any level. The fix adds `--cov-fail-under=90` to that exact invocation — right root cause, sound approach, correct layer (the CI integration job that actually runs coverage). The separate `pytest -q` at line 44 is the core/no-extras lane that intentionally does not measure coverage, so the floor's placement is correct and there is no second uninstrumented coverage run.

Task verification mandate satisfied: the floor IS present (=90) and current coverage EXCEEDS it. I ran the exact gated invocation locally: `python -m pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term --cov-fail-under=90` → "Required test coverage of 90% reached. Total coverage: 96.58%", 555 passed, 5 skipped. Floor 90 sits ~6.6pts below actual with headroom for environment-skipped (Postgres/Qdrant/Ollama) tests.

Regression test test_coverage_floor_is_enforced passes and DISCRIMINATES at the substring level: I reverted the fix (stripped ` --cov-fail-under=90` from ci.yml) and the test failed with "coverage measured but not enforced (vacuous gate)"; restoring made it green again, git tree clean.

Residual gap (why CONCERN, not CONFIRMED): the regression test asserts only that the literal `--cov-fail-under=` substring is present. It does NOT assert the threshold value, nor that the flag is attached to a `--cov=`-instrumented run. Consequently a silent weakening of the gate to `--cov-fail-under=0` (or `=1`) would re-introduce a vacuous gate yet keep this test green — the test does not protect the property it claims ("floor is enforced"), only that the flag literal exists. The fix itself is correct and currently effective; the gap is in the durability of the regression guard.
concerns: test_coverage_floor_is_enforced asserts only the substring '--cov-fail-under=' is present, not its value; a regression to --cov-fail-under=0 would re-create a vacuous gate while keeping the test green. Recommend asserting an integer threshold >= a minimum (e.g., parse the value and assert >= 90, or at minimum > 0).

The test does not verify the --cov-fail-under flag is on the same invocation as --cov=assay_engine; a future split could leave the floor flag on an uninstrumented run. Currently fine (both on line 110) but unguarded.

The floor is defined only inline in ci.yml, not in pyproject.toml [tool.coverage.report] fail_under, so local `python -m pytest` does not enforce it — enforcement exists solely in CI. Acceptable for the finding but worth noting for defense-in-depth.
